The simple allocation portfolios outperform SOR in almost every risk category. So, what is the problem if SOR has a better overall return? The problems stem from its rather high standard deviation (19.07%). Since this measure is used as a divisor for all of the risk measures, it lowers the results. At this point, it becomes a question of whether an investor is willing to stomach the sharper ups and downs this fund will have compared to its benchmarks.

My Take

Approaching SOR as a large-cap ETF like FactSet does will cause some confusion, which is why it is important to look at the underlying assets. Without doing so, one might be inclined to view SOR as a 3.5-Star fund. Its overperformance compared to the S&P 500 is appealing, even though the large deviation spread does cause concern.

So, how does one look at SOR? On average, it does minimize losses during down years (3.20% vs. -15.38%). That is an excellent result, but there is a rub. The worst 12-month period for SOR saw a loss of -55.13% (12 months ending 11/2008). That is a tremendous loss of capital for one to stomach for a moderately allocated portfolio, especially when the more traditional 70/30 or 60/40 portfolios limited their losses to half of that amount. On the same token, SOR saw a gain of 98.89% for the 12 months ending in March 2010. While one can say that was during a unique period in stock market history, these results are still part of SOR’s DNA.

SOR offers a compelling balance: strong returns, inflation resistance, and global diversification. However, its high standard deviation (19.07%) means investors must stomach sharper ups and downs compared to traditional allocation portfolios. If you prioritize growth and beating inflation, SOR is a solid pick. If minimizing volatility is your focus, a traditional 70/30 or 60/40 mix might be preferable.

Have fun and go make some money.

data.title: Duolingo: Playing Chess While Others Play Checkers
data.description:
Duolingo (NASDAQ:DUOL) has been a core holding of mine for some time yet I regretfully stated investors shouldn’t rush to add shares of Duolingo in my last article as I stated the company's valuation seemed lofty. Since that article, Duolingo has crushed the S&P 500 as it’s up by over 40%. YTD Duolingo is up over 60% while the S&P 500 is just barely positive.

In Q1 2025, Duolingo delivered yet another masterful quarter firmly illustrating that naysayers who think AI can displace this company, continue to be absolutely wrong.

Let’s dig into the most recent quarter as I discuss why despite having a high valuation, quality, high performing companies, like Duolingo, aren’t cheap for a reason.

Duo is Far From Dead

When a company’s CEO doesn’t even have prepared remarks, drops a joke, then just goes to Q&A, I think that’s a clear sign the quarter went well.

As it relates to user metrics, in Q1 2025 Duolingo had 46.6 million daily active users (DAUs) which is an increase of 49% year-over-year. Monthly active users came in at 130.2 million which is a 33% increase compared to Q1 2024. Paid subscribers were 10.3 million for the quarter which is an increase of 40% compared to the prior year first quarter. Clearly, these are impressive numbers that illustrate the power of the platform.

Part of the reason the company was able to keep this sustained growth and keep users engaged this quarter was with a very successful marketing campaign in which Duolingo’s mascot Duo the owl died (but thankfully came back to life). This campaign generated over 1.7 billion impressions for the company and as CEO Luis von Ahn stated on the earnings call, the cost of this campaign was “essentially nothing.”

In the quarter, Duolingo also launched chess which will also likely bring new users into the platform and continue to keep current users engaged. Impressively, Duolingo’s chess was first pitched by two employees (non-programmers even) who created a prototype with the help of AI. Here’s what von Ahn had to say about this new addition, “We think there is a huge demand for chess. There are hundreds of millions of people who want to play chess. And the same is true for math and music. I mean they’re growing really well. We are monetizing all of these subjects, by the way. The way you monetize them is the same way we monetize language learning, which is if you want to turn off ads, you can pay to subscribe. So we’re monetizing them already. But compared to language learning, they’re a lot smaller. But yes, we’re very excited about it.”

Clearly this is a positive for Duolingo and it shows the company can continue to add more subjects and categories outside of language learning. With the help of AI I believe Duolingo can continue to roll out more and more subjects in a similar fashion.

Financials

In Q1 2025 Duolingo had revenue of roughly $231 million, which is a 38% increase compared to Q1 2024. Total bookings were roughly $271 million, an increase of 38% compared to the prior year first quarter. Subscription bookings were roughly $232 million, which is a 40% increase compared to Q1 2024.

This revenue growth led to Duolingo posting net income of $35 million and earnings per share of basic share of common stock of $0.78, both notable improvements compared to the prior year as you can see below:

Duolingo is expecting bookings in the range of $243 to $247 million for Q2 2025 and revenue in the range of $238 million to $242 million. For fiscal year 2025, Duolingo expects bookings to be in the range of $1,117 million to $1,126 million. Revenue for the year is expected to be between $987 to $996 million, a year-over year of more than 30%. This illustrates Duolingo expects their strong bookings and revenue growth to continue.

Duolingo has maintained an excellent balance sheet with more than $883 million in cash and cash equivalents. Duolingo has more than enough current assets to cover all of their liabilities (most of which is deferred revenue) and the company has no long-term debt

Finally, from acash flow perspective in Q1 2025 cash flow from operating activities was roughly $106 million and free cash flow from roughly $103 million. This is compared to nearly $84 million of cash flow from operating activities and nearly $79 million in free cash flow in the prior year quarter.

Clearly, Duolingo continues to grow revenue at a stellar pace while increasing net income and cash flows while maintaining no long-term debt. Aside from some dilution due an increase in common stock, investors don’t have too much to complain about after this fantastic quarter.

No Stopping AI

Luis von Ahn has made it clear AI will continue to be a key part of Duolingo's future. The company’s CEO and co-founder made some arguably controversial comments recently about AI speaking about his company and education in general. First von Ahn stated he thought AI could replace contract workers within Duolingo, although he later walked back those comments. Next, von Ahn noted he believed AI was going to be a key part of education in schools. Although, he noted teachers will still be necessary as parents need childcare. I don’t disagree that teachers won’t easily be replaced as they play a key and important role in our communities. However, teaching is a very difficult job with changing curriculum, expanding classroom sizes, and challenging students. Teachers need help and that’s where AI can come in. I believe AI can help cater to specific students' needs and provide teachers with useful insights.

As it relates to Duolingo specifically, von Ahn and team are using AI and they seem to be using it well according to the CEO as on the latest earnings call he stated, “...AI is very transformative for our business. It helps us teach better. It helps us create content a lot faster. It helps us create content that was just infeasible to do before. I mean a good example is we just released 148 language courses. We wouldn’t have done that if it wasn’t for AI. We are really going all in as a company on AI, because it’s a technology that particularly applies to us, because the main improvement in AI in the last couple of years is large language models and language is what we teach. So, it particularly applies to us. So, you will see us – I mean we really are – a lot of features are being developed that are related to AI, and you will see us develop the right features for the users. So, we really want to stay ahead with it. And we believe that we are far ahead of everybody else on this.”

As von Ahn noted, it’s quite impressive that with the use of AI Duolingo was able to create 148 new courses within the span of a year. It took Duolingo 12 years to release 100 courses so it’s quite the feat that 148 were created in a single year. I imagine it will only get easier for Duolingo to continue this pace with the help of AI.

Max has continued to add features with the help of AI as well. For example, in video calls with Lily Duolingo is making Lily’s environment more “realistic” and they are working to create a more immersive, engaging experience. I think these are the type of features that will keep Max users engaged and likely get more users to convert. Plus, as von Ahn noted on the earnings call, the price of Max will come down in certain parts of the world (such as India) which will lead to continued growth in Max users.

Regarding risks, those with a bearish view of Duolingo typically feel AI is the biggest risk to Duolingo. Some feel ChatGPT and similar tools like Google’s Gemini may impact Duolingo’s business. As I’ve stated in prior articles, I don’t see ChatGPT as a direct threat to Duolingo because I think there is a difference between wanting to learn a language and wanting a translation.

I think the bigger threat to Duolingo is a competitor catching up to Duolingo with the help of AI. As stated in Duolingo’s most recent 10K filing, they view competition as one of their biggest risks, stating, “The online language learning industry is highly competitive, with low switching costs and a consistent stream of new products and entrants, and innovation by our competitors may disrupt our business.”

However, I think Duolingo has a first mover advantage in language learning, similar to Netflix’s first mover streaming advantage. Within their 10K, Duolingo’s biggest risk, which I agree with, is keeping existing customers engaged. To me that translates to Duolingo continuing to leverage AI and use it more effectively than their competition. Currently, I do believe Duolingo is doing that and von Ahn and team seem focused on continuing to heavily use AI in the future. Duolingo was never intended to have a model in which humans spoke with other humans. They have also had a tech first mindset which is why Duolingo has the best course content with the most engaging experience and this in turn has led to Duolingo continuing to have the most users within this niche industry.

Valuation

As you can see from the below valuation metrics, Seeking Alpha's overall value grade for Duolingo is a "F"

Although Duolingo is profitability I still believe price to sales is likely the best valuation metric for this growing company.The company's forward price to sales is 24.02 which is still certainly high and much higher than the sector median and if you look at the below graph you'll see Duolingo is at their highest prices to sales ratio in a year:

Certainly, that's a high multiple however the above chart was very similar to the one I posted back in December 2024. Premium companies garner premium valuations and like acclaimed investor and author Chris Mayer said, "Great stocks have a ready fan club, and many will spend of their time near 52-week highs, as you'd expect. It is rare to get a truly great business at dirt-cheap prices."

If you're paying for quality then you're paying up but in the long run it's worth it. In the case of top-notch companies like Duolingo, investors can dollar-cost average into a position without worrying about buying near all-time highs.

In looking at Duolingo's valuation from another viewpoint, I updated my reverse discounted cash flow model and with a discounted rate of 10% and a terminal rate of 3% you would need a growth rate of 26% to justify Duolingo’s current share price:

Now that's certainly a high growth rate but current analysts are estimating Duolingo's growth will meet if not exceed 26% in the next three years. Furthermore if you envision Duolingo continuing to launch new non-language courses like what the company just did with chess then I certainly think it's feasible Duolingo can continue to grow at these high rates as they launch then monetize new courses.

Conclusion

Duolingo delivered another stellar quarter as revenue, net income, MAUs, DAUs and paid subscribers all continued to grow.

I believe Duolingo’s moat within the language learning space is clearly intact and Duolingo’s use of AI seems far ahead of competitors. I don’t believe ChatGPT or similar artificial intelligence applications will impact Duolingo’s business.

Some would probably view Duolingo as an expensive stock yet I firmly believe premium businesses justify premium valuations and Duolingo has proven quarter after quarter this an elite business with much more growth coming in the years to come.

This is a business long-term investors can dollar cost average into and feel confident that over the years to come, Duolingo will continue to be an excellent investment for growth oriented investors.

data.title: FormFactor: The AI Thesis Has Yet To Deliver
data.description:
FormFactor (NASDAQ:FORM), a supplier of test and measuring solutions for the semiconductor industry, has continued to surrender some of its prior gains, which were accumulated thanks to FORM being perceived as a beneficiary of the boom in AI. The stock is now available for about half of what it went for at the peak in mid-2024 after selling off for much of 2025, but it may still be too early to be thinking of long FORM. The stock arguably does not, among other things, offer good enough value, at least not yet. Why will be covered next.

FORM may be caught in a downtrend

A previous article from October 2024 mentioned how FORM had greatly benefited from in essence becoming a play on artificial intelligence or AI due to being a supplier of equipment needed to produce HBM memory, which in turn is used along with GPUs for the training and inference AI requires. However, the article noted that this exposure to AI can cut both ways by pushing the stock higher, but also lower if the AI story does not play out the way expected. In the end, the article decided to rate FORM a hold.

The chart above shows how the stock has moved lower in the nine months that have passed. FORM peaked at $63.63 in July 2024, but the stock has moved lower since then with a series of lower highs, which are connected to form a descending upper trendline in the chart. The overall direction of the stock appears to be down based on this, which may discourage potential buyers of the stock.

More significantly, the stock is approaching the upper trendline. The significance of this is that whenever the stock came close to or into contact with this trendline, the stock proceeded to turn lower. This is what has happened in the last 1-2 months, but also December/January and July of last year, as shown in the chart. This suggests FORM needs to be treated with caution because a move downwards in the stock may be close at hand due to resistance, in line with the existing trend.

It’s also worth mentioning that the July 2024 high of $63.63 was the culmination of a long move upwards in the stock that started with a low of $18.15 in November 2022. The stock’s most recent low of $22.58 in early April came close, but did not quite reach the November 2022 low of $18.15. The stock has moved higher since early April, but if the stock wants to complete a 100% Fibonacci retracement of the prior move upwards with a move downwards, then the bottom may not yet be in. FORM could still be heading lower due to this.

Is FORM still an AI play?

FORM supplies probe cards and other products to more than just the DRAM industry, but it is the DRAM segment that has been the star performer for FORM, especially in recent quarters. This growth in the DRAM segment has been assisted by the ongoing industry transition towards DDR5 and LPDDR5, but the biggest driver of growth has been HBM memory, which is needed for AI applications.

Demand for HBM memory has been so strong that it can be said to be in short supply. DRAM manufacturers have been raising production to meet this demand, which in turn has benefited FORM because of the testing HBM requires. HBM is also much more test intensive than say regular DRAM for PCs or mobile devices, which further increases growth opportunities for FORM.

FORM can therefore still be seen as a play on the boom in AI, which is still going strong. With that said, there is no denying the decline in the stock in the past 12 months, especially in 2025, which was caused by several factors. FORM, for instance, has been affected by the worldwide tariff dispute, like other stocks. While FORM does most manufacturing in the U.S., it does import components used in various products, which means tariffs are relevant to FORM.

FORM has also been negatively affected by export controls, which resulted in lost sales to China in recent quarters. This has affected growth, which, combined with relatively high multiples the stock went for due to the long rally in 2022-2024, made it difficult for FORM to keep flying high and the stock had no choice but to move lower in response to the aforementioned headwinds.

Is FORM performing as billed?

Another factor that contributed to the recent decline in the stock is the fact that FORM is in a mini slump of sorts in terms of demand. FORM is supposed to be a growth stock with its exposure to AI, and all its potential use cases, which is the main reason why the stock price was bid up after the AI boom got going, but recent growth has struggled to move forward.

In the most recent report or Q1 FY2025 report, for instance, revenue declined sequentially for the second consecutive quarter to $171.4M, which resulted in GAAP EPS of $0.08 and non-GAAP EPS of $0.23. In comparison, FORM earned $0.28 and $0.19, respectively, on revenue of $168.7M in Q1 FY2024.

Guidance calls for revenue of $185-195M, GAAP EPS of $0.14-0.22 and non-GAAP EPS of $0.26-0.34 in Q2 FY2025. In comparison, FORM earned $0.25 and $0.35, respectively, on revenue of $197.5M in Q2 FY2024. In other words, growth has not exactly been roaring forward, but more on the flattish side and this has been the case for some time.

Earlier in the year FORM closed the books on FY2024 by reporting revenue of $764M, which is not much different from three years ago or FY2021 when revenue came in at $770M. What this shows is that AI has not been that much of a game changer thus far for FORM. AI-related revenue has definitely had an impact, specifically in terms of revenue related to HBM, but this has been offset by weakness elsewhere, in NAND and other segments, which resulted in flattish growth.

In fact, had it not been for HBM, recent results could have been significantly worse. All the growth in the current quarter, for instance, is expected to be due to HBM. From the Q1 FY2025 earnings call:

“really all the growth in DRAM is coming from HBM. We see the non-HBM, so DDR5, low power DDR5, a little bit of residual DDR4 continuing to be pretty steady, but flat at around $20 million a quarter, which historically for form factor is a cyclical low for the commodity DRAM business.

So, really, all the sequential growth Q1 to Q2 that we're projecting is due to HBM growth.”

Source: FORM earnings call

With the exception of HBM, the rest of the market is not that strong for FORM. The issue here is that FORM supplies products for more than just HBM, and if it is to grow at the pace the market would like it to, all segments need to perform well and contribute, not just one.

Is FORM worth buying at current levels?

FORM is available at less than half the asking price at the peak in mid-2024. Yet there is an argument to be made that the stock is still not worth buying despite the sizable price cut. According to the methodology favored by people like Peter Lynch, a stock needs to have a PEG ratio below one in order to be considered undervalued relative to expected growth, but it appears FORM does not meet this criteria.

Non-GAAP EPS reached $1.20 TTM, which confers FORM a P/E ratio of 26.1x with the stock closing at $31.36 on June 3. So in order for PEG to come in at less than one, FORM needs to grow earnings by more than 26.1% per year, on average, in the next five years. This looks like a difficult task because FORM has not managed to grow at that kind of pace.

Non-GAAP EPS reached $1.15 in the last completed fiscal or FY2024, up from $1.04 in FY2019, which means EPS grew at a CAGR of just 2% in FY2019-2024, or well below the more than 26.1% needed. If earnings grow by just 2% per year, on average, in the next five years or FY2024-2029, you get a PEG far above one. This suggests FORM is probably overvalued relative to the earnings growth it is likely to achieve.

Moreover, while it is not impossible for FORM to grow earnings at a faster pace in the coming years than in the past several years, it appears that even FORM does not think it can achieve a very high growth rate. The latest financial model presented by FORM targets non-GAAP EPS of $2.00. FORM did not state the exact timeframe FORM wants to achieve this target, but assuming the model refers to the next five years and FY2024 as the base, then EPS would need to grow at a CAGR of 11.7% in FY2024-2029. This is better than before, but not enough, because it implies a PEG ratio of well above one, which suggests FORM is still not a buy at current levels.

Investor takeaways

The halving of the stock price in the past 12 months can be attributed to several reasons. Export controls have resulted in lost sales in China. The global dispute about tariffs have not left FORM unscathed. Recent quarterly results have not lived up to FORM’s billing as a growth stock thanks to AI, which is problematic due to the relatively high multiples the stock is assigned. FORM still trades at 26x TTM earnings, even after the big reduction in the stock price.

FORM has certainly benefited from the strong demand for HBM memory, but other segments have not been as strong and DRAM, let alone HBM, revenue only makes up a minority of sales. This weakness in other segments has negated the growth due to HBM, which has resulted in flattish growth at FORM,. The flat results stand in contrast to FORM’s billing as a growth stock driven by AI demand, which is why the stock price was bid up in the first place.

The stock price has been cut in half, but if people like Peter Lynch are right, FORM still does not offer good value. FORM needs to grow EPS at a relatively fast pace of more than 26.1% per year in the next several years to offset the relatively high P/E ratio assigned to FORM and in order for PEG to come in at less than one, but there is reason to doubt if FORM can achieve this desired growth rate.

If FORM grows earnings in the coming years at the same pace it did in recent years, then FORM looks overvalued. FORM needs to do much better than it has done in recent years, but it seems that even FORM does not think it can grow earnings at a CAGR of more than 26.1% in order to get a PEG ratio of less than one. The financial model suggests a CAGR of no more than the mid teens is achievable. So even if FORM hits the targets set out in the current financial model, which is not guaranteed, FORM would still end up with a PEG of 2.2x. It can therefore be argued that FORM does not offer good enough value to be worth buying, despite the recent decrease in the price of the stock.

I am neutral on FORM with the above in mind. If FORM is to become a buy it needs to offer good enough value to entice buyers to step in. This requires much faster earnings growth, or, alternatively, if this is too difficult as suggested by the current financial model, a further reduction in the stock price. If the stock does head lower, which the charts suggest is a real possibility, then FORM needs to be re-evaluated. At this time, FORM is not worth a buy.
